active_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.optimize.minimize with trust-constr method and Latin Hypercube Sampling initialization
active_paradigms.description: Replace SLSQP with trust-constr method which showed proven improvement (+0.0029). Use scipy.stats.qmc.LatinHypercube for generating 30+ diverse initial configurations instead of random perturbations. The trust-constr method uses an interior-point approach that handles nonlinear constraints more robustly. Increase maxiter to 5000 for better convergence. Structure constraints using NonlinearConstraint with sparse Jacobian for efficiency. This directly builds on the proven success of trust-constr.
active_paradigms.what_to_optimize: sum of radii through better constraint handling and systematic global search coverage
active_paradigms.cautions: trust-constr is slower than SLSQP, balance number of trials vs evaluation timeout. Provide analytical constraint Jacobians if possible for faster convergence. Ensure bounds are properly set for all 78 variables (x, y, r for each circle).

active_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.optimize.differential_evolution with penalty method for global optimization
active_paradigms.description: Apply differential_evolution as a population-based global optimizer that can escape local optima where gradient methods get stuck. Define bounds for all 78 variables. Use penalty function: objective = -sum(radii) + penalty_weight * sum(max(0, constraint_violations)^2). Set strategy='best1bin', popsize=30, maxiter=2000, mutation=(0.5, 1.0), recombination=0.7, seed=42. Enable polish=True with SLSQP for local refinement of best solution. This evolutionary approach explores the solution space differently from gradient descent.
active_paradigms.what_to_optimize: sum of radii through global search that can discover better local optima unreachable by gradient methods
active_paradigms.cautions: Penalty weight must be carefully tuned - start with 1000 and adjust. Too low allows infeasible solutions, too high prevents exploration. Differential evolution is significantly slower than gradient methods, may need fewer iterations. Run multiple times with different seeds.

active_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.optimize.least_squares with combined residuals for constraint satisfaction and radius maximization
active_paradigms.description: Reformulate as nonlinear least squares: minimize ||residuals||^2 where residuals concatenate: (1) max(0, r_i - x_i) for left boundary, (2) max(0, x_i + r_i - 1) for right boundary, (3) similar for y boundaries, (4) max(0, r_i + r_j - dist_ij) for overlaps, (5) -sum(radii)/weight for maximization. Use method='trf' (trust region reflective) which handles bounds natively. Set ftol=1e-12, xtol=1e-12, max_nfev=50000. This least squares paradigm optimizes feasibility and objective simultaneously.
active_paradigms.what_to_optimize: sum of radii through residual minimization that naturally balances constraint satisfaction with objective maximization
active_paradigms.cautions: Weight for sum_radii residual must be carefully balanced - try values 10-100. The trf method requires finite bounds for all variables. Soft constraints via residuals may not achieve exact feasibility - add post-validation. Initial guess quality significantly affects convergence.

tried_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.spatial.distance.pdist for vectorized contact constraint computation with iterative radius expansion
tried_paradigms.description: Instead of optimizing all variables simultaneously, use an iterative expansion approach. Start with small equal radii circles in good positions. At each iteration: compute all pairwise distances using pdist (vectorized, fast), identify the most constrained circle (smallest gap to neighbors/walls), expand that circle's radius until it contacts something, then recompute distances. This mimics physical bubble growth and naturally finds good contact topologies. Use scipy.spatial.Delaunay to identify neighbor relationships for efficient gap computation. The iterative nature ensures each circle reaches its maximum possible radius given the constraints.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: each circle's radius independently in sequence, building a dense contact network
tried_paradigms.cautions: Need to handle the order of expansion carefully (larger circles first often works better), must recompute distances after each expansion, boundary constraints need explicit checking

tried_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.optimize.minimize with COBYLA method for constraint-based optimization
tried_paradigms.description: COBYLA (Constrained Optimization BY Linear Approximation) handles non-linear constraints differently than SLSQP - it builds linear approximations of constraints and objective. This can sometimes find better solutions in highly non-convex landscapes. Use the same corner-heavy initialization but with COBYLA's derivative-free approach which may navigate the constraint landscape differently. Set maxiter to 3000 and use rhobeg=0.05 for initial step size.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: sum of radii by potentially finding different local optima
tried_paradigms.cautions: COBYLA doesn't support bounds directly, so bounds must be encoded as inequality constraints. Use larger maxiter since COBYLA may need more iterations. The method is derivative-free which can be advantageous for non-smooth regions.

tried_paradigms.idea: Direct hexagonal lattice construction with boundary adaptation
tried_paradigms.description: Construct packing directly from geometric principles: (1) Place 4 corner circles with radius computed to maximize space (2) Fill edges with circles arranged in hexagonal contact pattern along each side (3) Fill interior with hexagonal lattice packing, computing exact positions using sqrt(3)/2 vertical spacing (4) Compute radii to maximize contacts - each interior circle touches 6 neighbors. Use analytical formulas for positions rather than optimization, then do a single refinement pass.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: packing density through optimal geometric arrangement
tried_paradigms.cautions: Hexagonal packing in a square requires careful boundary handling. The lattice spacing must be computed to fit 26 circles optimally. May need to adjust lattice orientation or offset to better fit the square container.

tried_paradigms.idea: Sequential greedy placement with maximum radius computation
tried_paradigms.description: Build packing incrementally: (1) Place 4 corner circles at maximum possible radius (r = 1/(2+sqrt(2)) ≈ 0.293 if all equal, but optimize individually) (2) For each subsequent circle, compute the maximum radius it can have given all previously placed circles (3) Place each new circle at the position that maximizes its radius (4) Use scipy.optimize.minimize for each single-circle placement subproblem. This greedy approach avoids the complexity of optimizing all 78 variables simultaneously.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: each circle's radius independently while respecting constraints
tried_paradigms.cautions: Greedy placement order matters significantly - place larger circles first. The subproblems are simpler (3 variables each) but the global optimum may differ from greedy solution. Try different placement orders (corners→edges→interior vs other orderings).

tried_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.spatial.Voronoi for geometric radius computation
tried_paradigms.description: Transform the problem by computing radii geometrically from Voronoi diagram instead of optimizing them. Design initial positions with corner-heavy asymmetric pattern (4 large corners, 12 edges, 10 interior in hexagonal arrangement). Compute scipy.spatial.Voronoi diagram for these positions. For each circle, set radius to the minimum of: half the distance to each Voronoi neighbor (where circles would touch) and distance to the square boundary (x, 1-x, y, 1-y). This guarantees valid packing by construction - no overlaps possible. The positions can be designed to maximize the resulting Voronoi-computed radii, exploiting the geometric relationship between position spacing and achievable radius.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: Circle positions designed to maximize Voronoi-computed radii; radii are computed deterministically from geometry
tried_paradigms.cautions: Handle Voronoi cells at square boundaries correctly - finite Voronoi computation needs boundary reflection or clipping; ensure radius computation accounts for both Voronoi neighbors and square edges; the quality depends on position design

tried_paradigms.description: Replace constraint-based optimization with penalty-based optimization using L-BFGS-B, which is faster and may find different local optima. Formulate objective as -sum(radii) + penalty_coeff * (overlap_penalty + boundary_penalty), where overlap_penalty = sum of max(0, r_i + r_j - distance_ij)^2 for all pairs and boundary_penalty = sum of max(0, x-r, r-1+x, y-r, r-1+y)^2. L-BFGS-B handles 78 variables efficiently and the penalty formulation avoids sqrt numerical issues. Use increasing penalty coefficients: start with penalty=100, run optimization, then increase penalty and restart from the solution to enforce constraints more strictly.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: All 78 variables (positions and radii) with penalty-weighted objective function
tried_paradigms.cautions: Penalty coefficient must be large enough to make constraint violations costly; final solution must have near-zero penalty to be valid; use multiple random initializations like current approach; L-BFGS-B only handles box constraints, so other constraints must be penalties

tried_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.optimize.fmin_cobyla with linear constraint approximation
tried_paradigms.description: Apply COBYLA (Constrained Optimization BY Linear Approximation) which handles constraints differently from SLSQP. COBYLA builds linear models of constraints in a trust region, while SLSQP uses quadratic approximations - this different strategy may find different local optima. Formulate constraints as functions returning positive values for feasible points: for each pair, constraint = distance - r_i - r_j; for boundaries, constraint = x - r, constraint = 1 - x - r, etc. Use corner-heavy initial placement. COBYLA is particularly suited for problems with many constraints (325+ here) and may navigate the constraint landscape more efficiently than SLSQP's quadratic approach.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: All 78 variables with explicit constraint functions evaluated at each iteration
tried_paradigms.cautions: COBYLA constraint functions must return positive for feasible, negative for infeasible; use rhobeg parameter (initial step size) around 0.05-0.1; may need more iterations than SLSQP (set maxfun high); use multiple starting points as COBYLA is still a local method

tried_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.optimize.minimize with trust-constr method for better handling of non-linear distance constraints
tried_paradigms.description: The trust-constr method uses a trust-region approach that handles non-convex constraints more robustly than SLSQP's sequential quadratic programming. Replace the SLSQP method with trust-constr, which maintains a trust region around the current iterate and solves a constrained subproblem. Use the same constraint formulation but specify method='trust-constr' with options like {'verbose': 2, 'maxiter': 3000, 'gtol': 1e-8}. The trust-region approach is particularly effective for the quadratic distance constraints between circles, potentially finding better local optima that SLSQP misses.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: sum of radii through better constraint handling
tried_paradigms.cautions: trust-constr requires sparse Jacobian for efficiency with 78 variables. Use tr_solver='exact' for small problems or 'lsmr' for larger ones. Set appropriate initial trust radius with options['initial_tr_radius']. The method can be slower per iteration but may converge to better solutions.

tried_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.spatial.Voronoi for analytical radius computation to reduce problem dimension
tried_paradigms.description: Exploit geometric structure: given positions, maximum radii are analytically determined by Voronoi cells. For each circle center, compute the Voronoi diagram, then the maximum radius equals min(distance to unit square boundary, half-distance to each Voronoi neighbor). This reduces optimization to 52 position variables instead of 78. Define objective function that: (1) computes Voronoi cells via scipy.spatial.Voronoi, (2) calculates max radii analytically, (3) returns negative sum. Use scipy.optimize.minimize with bounds on positions only. The reduced dimensionality and geometric structure can lead to better solutions.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: positions only with radii computed geometrically
tried_paradigms.cautions: Voronoi computation fails for degenerate configurations (colinear points). Add small random jitter to positions before Voronoi computation. Handle boundary circles specially - their Voronoi cells extend outside the square. Use scipy.spatial.Voronoi with qhull_options='Qbb Qc Qz' for robustness.

tried_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.optimize.least_squares with soft constraint formulation for different optimization landscape
tried_paradigms.description: Reformulate as a least-squares problem instead of constrained optimization. Create residual vector: (1) boundary violations as positive residuals when circle extends outside square, (2) overlap violations as positive residuals when circles overlap, (3) negative radius sum scaled by a factor. Use scipy.optimize.least_squares with bounds on variables. This creates a smoother optimization landscape where the solver can 'slide' along constraint boundaries. The soft constraint approach allows temporary violations during optimization that get resolved, potentially finding solutions that hard constraint methods miss.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: balance between constraint satisfaction and radius maximization
tried_paradigms.cautions: Scale residuals appropriately - boundary/overlap violations should be comparable to the radius sum term. Use loss='soft_l1' for robustness to outliers. Set ftol and xtol carefully (1e-10) for precision. The weighting between constraint violations and radius sum is critical; start with equal weights and adjust if needed.
